mnemonics.title: ALTER Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Remember ALTER: A-Add, L-Look (rename), T-Transform (modify), E-Erase (drop), R-Remove constraints. The word ALTER itself helps remember the sequence of operations.

mnemonics.title: DROP vs TRUNCATE vs DELETE
mnemonics.content: DROP = Destroy (structure + data), TRUNCATE = Throw away rows (keep structure), DELETE = Destroy rows one by one (can undo). Think: D-estroy, T-rows, D-ata

acronyms.usage: Commands that define database structure: CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE, RENAME. These auto-commit and cannot be rolled back.
Commands that manipulate data: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE. These can be wrapped in transactions and rolled back.
Standard language used to manage and manipulate relational databases.
Constraint that creates a relationship between tables. Must be dropped before dropping referenced columns.

visualTips.visualization: Think of a table as a building. ALTER is like renovation: ADD adds new rooms, MODIFY changes room sizes, DROP removes walls, RENAME changes room names.
DROP = Demolish the entire building. TRUNCATE = Empty all rooms but keep the building structure. DELETE = Remove furniture one piece at a time.
Think of foreign keys as chains connecting tables. You cannot break a link (drop column) without first unchaining (dropping FK) connected tables.

analogies.analogy: Like renovating a house - you can add rooms (columns), change room sizes (modify data types), or remove walls (drop columns) while keeping the house (table) intact.
Like demolishing a building - everything (structure and contents) is permanently destroyed. No recovery without backup.
Like emptying a swimming pool - the pool structure remains but all water (data) is removed quickly. Can be filled again.
Like adding rules to a club membership - once added, all members (rows) must follow these rules. Existing data must comply.
